title: Arbitrary norm growth in the 3D Navier-Stokes equations

thm:1.1.summary: For any non-zero $\Theta_*$, $\eta_*$ with $\Theta_*\cdoteta_*=0$, any $\epsilon_*>0$ and $n_max$, there exists smooth divergence-free $u^0$ with â€–$u^0$â€–$_{B^{-1}_{\infty,1}} \le 10^5$ whose NSE global strong solution approximates $\Theta_*sin(x\cdoteta_*)exp(-|\eta_*|^2t)$ with $C^{n_max}$ error at most $\epsilon_*$ on the interval $[1/2|\eta_*|^{-2}, 2|\eta_*|^{-2}]$. This is the norm growth construction.

rem:1.2.summary: The bound in thm:1.1 is in $B^{-1}_{\infty,1} (stronger$ than $BMO^{-1})$. Also notes $BMO^{-1} \cap C^\infty \subsetVMO^{-1}$ where Koch-Tataru local well-posedness applies. The constant 10^5 is not optimized.

rem:1.3.summary: Thm:1.1 and cor:1.5 likely extend to data bounded in $B^{-1}_{\infty,1}$ and small in $B^{-1}_{\infty,\infty}$ by combining with Bourgain-PavloviÄ‡ ideas, but this is left to future work.

rem:1.4.summary: Thm:1.1 says data from a bounded set can be chosen so that u approximates any prescribed global solution at a particular time scale; likely strengthens to u scattering to any global mild solution.

cor:1.5.summary: For any T>0 and non-decreasing f, the following hypothetical a priori estimates all fail: (1) â€–u(t)â€–$_{BMO^{-1}} \le$ f(â€–$u^0$â€–$_{BMO^{-1}}), (2) t^{(1+n)/2}$â€–$\nabla^n$ u(t)â€–$_\infty \le$ f(â€–$u^0$â€–), (3) localized enstrophy bound, (4) Prodi-Serrin-Ladyzhenskaya norms â€” ruling out all scale-invariant a priori estimates.

rem:1.6.summary: Cor:1.5 holds with $B^{-1}_{\infty,1}$ on the RHS; in fact even estimates from $B^{-1}_{\infty,1}$ data to $B^{-1}_{\infty,\infty}$ fail â€” growth occurs in the weakest critical translation-invariant space.

rem:1.7.summary: The four estimate types in cor:1.5 are not exhaustive â€” the same failure holds for essentially any scale-invariant measure. Discusses analogy with Strichartz estimates for critical dispersive equations.

cor:1.8.summary: For any T, R>0, there exists data $u^0 \in B_{VMO^{-1}}(0, r_0) \cap C^\infty$ for which the Picard iteration map $\Phi$ fails to converge in $B_{X_T}(0$, R). Proves Picard method is not robust in the well-posed class.

rem:2.1.summary: Order of parameter choices: $\gamma$ and b free (subject to constraints), then k_* large depending on $|\Theta_*|$, then A large depending on all others. Also explains why controlling $n_max$ derivatives requires tracking $n_max' \gg n_max$ throughout the proof.

lem:2.2.summary: Nash lemma: there exist $\theta_1,...,\theta_6 \in Q^3 \cap S^2$ and smooth $\Gamma_j \in C^\infty(B$; [1/5,1]) on the ball B = B(Id, 1/7) of symmetric $3\times3$ matrices such that M $= sum_j \Gamma_j(M)^2 \theta_j$ âŠ— $\theta_j$ for all M $\in$ B. Key algebraic tool enabling the Mikado flow ansatz to cancel leading-order NSE nonlinearity.

def:2.3.summary: Mikado flow geometry: oscillation directions $\eta_j (from {e_1,e_2,e_3}$ with $\eta_j\cdottheta_j=0)$, mollifiers $\phi_k$ at scales $ell_k = N_k^{-3/4}N_{k+1}^{-1/4}$, positions $(x_j)$, cylinders $C_j(r)$, and profiles $\phi_j \in C^\infty_c(C_j(\delta_0))$ satisfying shear-flow and normalization conditions.

lem:2.4.summary: Explicit positions $(x_j)^6_{j=1}$ satisfying def:2.3 exist with $\delta_0 = 1/15$. Verified by computing pairwise distances between periodic lines $L_j$, the smallest being $(8(49-15\pi))/(5*sqrt(481)) \approx 0.137 > 201/100 * 1/15$.

def:2.5.summary: Multi-scale velocity field construction: oscillatory profiles $\Psi_{j,k}(x,t) = \phi_j(M_k x) sin(N_k \eta_j\cdotx) e^{-N_k^2t}$, vector potentials $\psi_k$, and frequency-localized components $v_k(x,t) = C_k(1-e^{-2N_{k+1}^2t})$ P $\Delta\psi_k$. Full field v $= sum_k v_k$, with special forms at k=0 and k=k_*.

rem:2.6.summary: Explains rationale of def:2.5: the $(1-e^{-2N_{k+1}^2t})$ factor reflects k-th scale activation; mollification in $\psi_k$ avoids derivative loss in lem:2.7; shear flow property kills cross-term divergences; $\Psi_{j,k}$ solves heat equation to leading order.

lem:2.7.summary: Main inductive construction: existence of $a_{j,k} \in C^\infty(T^3)$ satisfying the matrix equation $sum_j B_{j,k} a_{j,k}^2(x) \theta_j$âŠ—$\theta_j = -4N_{k-1}$ D $\psi_{k-1}^0 + p_k$ Id (which encodes the cascade mechanism), plus norm bounds $1 \le a_{j,k} \le 32000$ and $c_*^{2^{-k}} N_k^{-1} \le$ â€–D $\psi_k^0$â€–$_\infty \leC\cdotN_k^{-1} (repeated$ squaring growth). The Nash lemma is applied inductively to solve the matrix equation at each scale.

lem:2.8.summary: Decay bounds: â€–$\nabla^n v_k(t)$â€–$_\infty$ â‰˛ $(|\Theta_*|/|\eta_*|)^{2^{-k}} N_k^{1+n} e^{-N_k^2t}$ for n $\le n_max'$, and the global bound â€–$t^{1/2-1/p}$ vâ€–$_{L^p_t L^\infty_x}$ â‰˛$_{\Theta_*, k_*} 1$ for all p $\in [1,\infty]$. Both follow from lem:2.7 via Leibniz rule.

def:2.9.summary: Defines initial data $u^0 = v(\cdot,0) \in C^\infty(T^3)$, the local-in-time strong NSE solution u from $u^0$, maximal time T_*, and the error w(t,x) = u(t,x) - v(t,x) for t $\in [0,T_*)$.

prop:2.10.summary: Defect equation: v satisfies $\partial_t$ v $- \Deltav +$ P div(vâŠ—v) = P div f for a smooth forcing f obeying $t^{(2+n+\alpha)/2}$â€–$\nabla^n$ f(t)â€–$_{C^\alpha}$ â‰˛ $A^{-\beta}(N_0^2t)^{\alpha/2+\beta} e^{-N_0^2t}$. The force f collets all error terms from the cascade; its smallness $(O(A^{-\beta}))$ is the main technical output.

prop:2.11.summary: Smallness of error and globality: $T_* = \infty$ and $t^{(1+n)/2}$â€–$\nabla^n$ w(t)â€–$_\infty \le\epsilon (N_0^2t)^\beta$ for t $\in (0$, $KN_0^{-2}]$, all n $\le n_max'''$. Proved via Duhamel formula, GrĂ¶nwall with singular kernel, and a bootstrap argument using Kato's mild solution theory to extend $T_* = \infty$.

edges.note: Globality $T_*=\infty$ and the error bound on $\nabla^n$ E follow directly from prop:2.11; without it the solution might blow up before the claimed time interval.

Bounds â€–$\nabla^n v_k(t)$â€–$_\infty$ from lem:2.8 are used in the final estimate of â€–$\nabla^n$ Eâ€–$_\infty$ on $[|\eta_*|^{-2}, 2|\eta_*|^{-2}]$ to show each $v_k$ contributes negligibly for $k\ge1$.
Coefficient estimates from lem:2.7 are used to bound â€–$P_N v_{k_*}(0)$â€–$_\infty$ and ultimately â€–$u^0$â€–$_{B^{-1}_{\infty,1}} \le 10^5$.
$u^0$, u, and w are defined in def:2.9; the proof of thm:1.1 directly references these objects.

Applies thm:1.1 with $\Theta_*=ne_1$, $\eta_*=me_2$ for each $n,m\inN$, producing global solutions $u_{n,m}$ with â€–$u_{n,m}^0$â€–$_{B^{-1}_{\infty,1}} =$ O(1) but â€–$u_{n,m}(m^{-2})$â€–$_{BMO^{-1}}$ â‰ł n/m $- 1 \to \infty$.
Uses the same sequence $u_{n,m}$ from cor:1.5 to show the Picard iterate must leave any ball $B_{X_T}(0,R)$ since â€–$u_{n,m}(m^{-2})$â€–$_\infty$ â‰ł n - 1.
w satisfies $\partial_t$ w $- \Deltaw +$ P div(wâŠ—w + 2vâŠ™w) = -P div f, where f is the defect from prop:2.10; smallness of f drives smallness of w via GrĂ¶nwall.
The $L^p_t L^\infty_x$ bound on v from lem:2.8 controls the bilinear term vâŠ™w in the GrĂ¶nwall argument; also â€–vâ€–$_{L^2_t L^\infty_x}$ appears explicitly in the exponential factor.
w = u - v is defined in def:2.9; the Duhamel formula and bootstrap are set up for this object.

Error estimates for $E_{1,k}, E_{2,k}, R_k^{high}$ all require the coefficient bounds â€–$\nabla^n a_{j,k}$â€–$_\infty$ and â€–D $\psi_k^0$â€–$_\infty$ from lem:2.7; the key cancellation in prop:2.10 relies on the inductive formula (lem:2.7, eq 21).
The proof decomposes $v_k = v_k^p + v_k^e$ using the structure of def:2.5, and the anti-divergence operator R acts on P $div(v_k^p$ âŠ— $v_k^p)$ to isolate low- and high-frequency error terms.
The stationary phase estimate for R (eq 37) and the Calderon-Zygmund boundedness of $\nablaR$ are invoked; the operator R acts on Mikado structures built from the $\theta_j$ of lem:2.2.
Decay bounds â€–$\nabla^n v_k(t)$â€–$_\infty$ from lem:2.8 are used in the cross-term estimates for $sum_{k1\nek2} v_{k1}$âŠ—$v_{k2}$ and for $v_k^e$.
Proof of lem:2.8:9 (eq 32) is immediate from lem:2.7:27 (bounds on $\nabla^n \psi_k)$ via Leibniz rule with P $\Delta = -curl$ curl; higher derivative estimates use lem:2.7:27:1 and lem:2.7:27:2.
$v_k$ is defined in def:2.5 as $C_k(1-e^{-2N_{k+1}^2t})$ P $\Delta\psi_k$; the proof directly substitutes this definition.

The $\Gamma_j$ from Nash lemma define $a_{j,k}$ inductively: $a_{j,k}(x) = (28 B_{j,k}^{-1} N_{k-1}$ â€–D $\psi_{k-1}^0$â€–$_\infty)^{1/2} \Gamma_j(Id -$ D $\psi_{k-1}^0 / (7$â€–D $\psi_{k-1}^0$â€–$_\infty))$. This is the core algebraic step that cancels $R_{k+1}^{low}$.
$\psi_k$ and $\Psi_{j,k}$ are defined in def:2.5; lem:2.7 constructs the coefficient functions $a_{j,k}$ that make these vector potentials satisfy the inductive matrix equation.

The mollifiers $\phi_k$ and scale $ell_k$ from def:2.3 appear in the derivative estimates for $\psi_k$; the shear flow property (def:2.3 eq 17) and normalization (def:2.3 eq 18) are used to bound $E_1$ and identify the leading term.

def:2.5 uses oscillation directions $\eta_j$, mollifiers $\phi_k$, Mikado profiles $\phi_j$, and cylinders $C_j$ from def:2.3 to build the vector potentials $\psi_k$ and oscillatory profiles $\Psi_{j,k}$.
$\theta_j$ from the Nash lemma (lem:2.2) appear as the transport directions in the vector potentials: $\psi_k = N_k^{-2} \phi_k * sum_j a_{j,k} \Psi_{j,k} \theta_j$.
Oscillation directions $\eta_j$ are chosen from ${e_1,e_2,e_3}$ satisfying $\eta_j\cdottheta_j=0$, where $\theta_j \in Q^3 \cap S^2$ are the directions from the Nash lemma (lem:2.2).
Establishes that concrete positions $(x_j)^6_{j=1}$ exist satisfying the spatial separation condition (def:2.3 eq 15) with $\delta_0 = 1/15$, by explicit computation of pairwise line distances.

layer_1_algebraic_tools.description: Nash lemma and Mikado flow geometry â€” the algebraic foundations enabling the inverse cascade

layer_2_cascade_construction.description: Multi-scale velocity field definition â€” the principal ansatz for the norm growth solution

layer_3_inductive_cascade.description: Coefficient construction and cascade estimates â€” the heart of the inverse cascade via repeated squaring

layer_4_perturbation.description: Closeness of u to v: defect equation and bootstrap â€” showing the constructed v is a good approximate solution

layer_5_main_results.description: Main theorem, a priori estimate failure, and Picard divergence â€” the consequences of norm growth
